segments.s444-g65: همه سیستان را بیاراستند is a past-tense verb clause with the definite object marker را after the object. همه سیستان (lit. 'the whole Sistan') is the direct object followed by را; بیاراستند is the past 3rd-person plural (بی- + arast-and) meaning 'they adorned/arrayed'. Word order is Object + را + Verb, common in Persian.
segments.s444-g66: برین بر نهادند و برخاستند contains an older concatenated demonstrative and verb sequence. برین = بر + ین (classical spelling of بر این, 'upon these/this'); نهادند is the past 3rd-plural of نهادن 'to place/set (on)'. The second بر belongs with نهادن ('نهادن بر' = 'to place upon'). وَ (و) links to برخاستند, another past 3rd-plural 'they rose/they got up'. The subject is carried over from the previous clause (= they).
segments.s444-g67: به شبگیر برخاست آوای کوس uses به + temporal noun شبگیر to mean 'at/with the coming of night' (classical temporal expression). برخاست is the past 3rd-singular 'rose/arose', and آوای کوس is the subject phrase 'the sound/voice of the drum' (آوای = 'sound/voice' in the ezafe construction with کوس). Word order here is temporal phrase + verb + subject for poetic effect.
segments.s444-g68: شد از گرد لشکر سپهر آبنوس is a verbless/elliptical descriptive clause where شد ('happened/became') links the preceding action to the ensuing image. از گرد لشکر = 'from/about/around the host' (از + گرد 'around' + لشکر 'army'), and سپهر آبنوس is a poetic noun phrase 'ebony sky/heavens' (سپهر 'sky' qualified by آبنوس 'ebony/very dark'). Grammatically, شد predicates that condition (the heavens turning dark) applies with reference to the host.
segments.s444-g69: همی رفت زان لشکر نامدار uses همی as a classical imperfective/progressive particle ('was/were ...ing' or 'kept on'), followed by رفت (past of رفتن 'to go/ride forth'). زان = از + آن (classical 'from that') introduces source 'from that host', and نامدار is an adjective 'renowned' modifying لشکر. Word order is adverbial particle + verb + source phrase + modifier.
segments.s444-g70: سواران شمشیرزن صد هزار is a noun phrase: سواران 'horsemen' modified by شمشیرزن 'sword‑wielding/swordsmen', with the numeral phrase صد هزار 'a hundred thousand' giving the count (positioned after in Persian poetic order). There is no main verb here because this phrase completes the previous clause (they rode forth ... a hundred thousand horsemen).
segments.s444-g49: اگر (if) introduces a conditional clause. بشمری is the present/subjunctive/optative form of شمردن used here as 'you reckon/consider' (verb agrees with implied 2nd person). در جهان نامدار is a prepositional phrase 'in the world, renowned' where در + جهان = 'in the world' and نامدار (postpositive adjective) qualifies an implied noun ('renowned [one(s)]'); word order places the place (در جهان) before the adjective for poetic emphasis.
segments.s444-g50: سواری نبینی is a negative present-subjunctive/imperative construction: نبینی (you will not see / you should not see) negates دیدن; چو = 'like/as' introduces the comparison; اسفندیار is the standard proper name in the comparative phrase 'like Esfandiyār'. Overall the clause states the consequence of the condition (you will not see a rider like Esfandiyār).
segments.s444-g51: چه بیند is a nominal/relative-style phrase 'what he sees' (بیند is 3rd-person present/subjunctive of دیدن used here as a reported or prospective 'he sees'). و = 'and' links clauses. این را (this + object marker) marks the following object; چه پاسخ دهید is 'what answer you give' — چه = 'what', پاسخ = 'answer', دهید = 2nd-person plural/formal present/subjunctive of دادن (here used as 'you will give' or a polite imperative). The clause asks/notes both what he perceives and what reply you will make.
segments.s444-g52: بکوشید is the 2nd-person plural/formal imperative/subjunctive of کوشیدن 'strive/try'. تا here means 'so that / in order that'. رای is 'plan/counsel' (the thing to be established). فرخ in classical usage can be an epithet or name but here modifies رای (an attributive) or stands for a proper name; the sequence رای فرخ is 'Farrokh's counsel' or 'fortunate counsel'. نهید is the 2nd-person plural subjunctive/imperative of نهادن 'to place, to set', used as 'set/establish (it)'. The clause is a directive: strive in order to put that counsel into effect.
segments.s444-g53: چو = 'when'. بشنید is past 3rd-person singular of شنیدن 'he/it heard' (placed before its objects by poetic inversion). گفتار بهمن is the object 'Bahman's speech' (گفتار 'speech' followed by بهمن as possessive/name). سپاه ('host/army') here is the subject appearing after the object because of inverted poetic word order: 'When the army heard Bahman's speech...'.
segments.s444-g54: هرانکس که introduces a relative clause 'whoever/anyone who'. The remainder is a verbless nominal predicate common in Persian: بد شاه را Nِیکخواه — شاه is the noun 'king', را is the definite object/topic marker attached to شاه, بد is an epithet 'bad/ill' placed before شاه, and نیکخواه is a predicate noun 'well-wisher' (with the copula omitted). Syntactically it yields 'whoever is a well-wisher of (the) king' (the ordering and omission of 'است' are normal in classical poetic style).
segments.s444-g55: به آواز گفتند places the manner/medium first: به آواز 'aloud/with a voice', followed by گفتند (3rd-person plural past of گفتن) 'they said'. ما بنده‌ایم is a present-state declaration 'we are servants' where ما is the subject and بنده‌ایم is the predicate; the copular form is fused in the present plural ending -ایم. The whole clause reports spoken reply—'they said aloud: we are servants.'
segments.s444-g56: همه دل به مهر تو آگنده‌ایم is a single nominal clause with a plural predicate: همه 'all/everyone' + دل 'heart' (collective) + به مهر تو 'with love for you' (prepositional phrase where به marks the emotion) and آگنده‌ایم (we are filled) is the compound predicate (آگنده + -ایم), literally 'we are filled.' Word order places the quantifier and theme before the predicate for emphasis: 'We are all hearts filled with your love.'
segments.s444-g33: This is a preposed accompaniment phrase modifying the subject. منوچهر is the proper-name subject; با + (سلم و تور) marks accompaniment 'with Salm and Tur'. سترگ is an epithet after تور (poetic word order), agreeing as an attributive adjective meaning 'mighty/large' (poetic word-order places adjectives after the noun). Note سلم here functions as a proper name (not the common noun senses listed).
segments.s444-g34: بیاورد is a past narrative form 'brought/caused to bring' (3rd sg past narrative). ز آمل is a sep. prepositional phrase 'from Amol' (ز = from). سپاهی بزرگ is the direct object: سپاهی (army / troop) modified by بزرگ 'great/large'. Word order is Subject (implicit from prev. segment) + verb + adjuncts + object.
segments.s444-g35: به چین رفت is a simple past clause: به + place = 'to/into Chin', رفت = 'he went'. The following coordinate clause (introduced by و) is compressed: کین نیا بازخواست is a verbless/nominal cluster meaning 'the ancestor's vengeance called for/accounted for (retribution)' — بازخواست functions as a noun 'demand/reproach' with کین نیا as its genitive/possessive phrase. Poetic narrative often omits an explicit copula or auxiliary verb in such nominal clauses.
segments.s444-g36: مرا همچنان داستانست راست is a comment/clause giving the speaker's assessment. مرا is the first-person object/experiential pronoun 'to me' (literally 'me' but functioning as 'for me'); همچنان 'thus / as it has been / still'; داستانست is a fused predicate 'is a tale' (داستان + است) and راست 'true' modifies the predicate. Word order places the experiencer (مرا) up front for emphasis: 'to me, such a story is true.'
segments.s444-g37: چو ... آمد is a temporal clause: چو = 'when' and آمد = past 'came'. از افراسیاب is the source phrase 'from Afrasiab' (از = from). The whole segment sets time/location for the following action.
segments.s444-g38: This clause states the effect: ز خون کرد = 'because of/from blood he made' (ز = from/because of; کرد = made/caused). گیتی is the direct object 'the world'; چو دریای آب is a simile 'like a sea of water' (چو = like/as). The past verb کرد governs the whole predicate: 'he made the world like a sea (by) blood.'
segments.s444-g39: پدرم آمد is 'my father came' (پدر + possessive suffix -م = 'my father'; آمد = past 'came'). The coord. clause و کین لهراسپ خواست: کین is 'vengeance', لهراسپ is the name in genitive/possessive relation (the target or possessor), and خواست is past 'desired/sought/wanted' — together meaning 'and he sought vengeance on/against Lohrasp' (poetic genitive relation is flexible: either 'the vengeance of Lohrasp' or 'vengeance against Lohrasp'; context favors vengeance aimed at Lohrasp).
segments.s444-g40: ز کشته زمین کرد is a result clause: ز = 'from/because of'; کشته (pl./collective noun) = 'the slain' or 'those killed'; زمین = 'the earth/ground' (here the place on which they lie); کرد = 'made/caused' (past). با کوه راست is an instrumental / simile phrase 'with/as a mountain straight/upright' — با = 'with/by', کوه = 'mountain', راست = 'straight/upright' (here used as an image: he made a heap of corpses like an upright mountain). Overall: 'he made the slain into/on the earth like an upright mountain.'
segments.s444-g17: دو جنگی چو نوش‌آذر و مهرنوش — 'دو جنگی' is a nominal phrase 'two warriors/fighters' (noun before modifier is common in poetry). 'چو' here is the comparative 'like/as', linking the following proper names as examples or likenesses; نوش‌آذر and مهرنوش are proper names, joined by و 'and'. The whole segment sets up a simile: 'two warriors, like Nush-āzar and Mehrnush.'
segments.s444-g18: که از درد ایشان برآمد خروش — 'که' is the relative/connector 'who/that', introducing the clause describing those two. 'از' = 'out of/from', 'درد' here denotes emotional sorrow/affliction causing an outcry, and 'ایشان' is the archaic polite/3pl 'they/them' referring back to the two. 'برآمد' is a past/imperfective narrative 'arose/issued forth', and 'خروش' is the noun 'cry/roar'. Together: 'from their grief a cry arose.'
segments.s444-g19: چو اسفندیاری که اندر جهان — 'چو' = 'as/like' introducing a comparison to Esfandiyār (proper name). 'که' is the relative 'who/that' leading into the descriptive clause; 'اندر جهان' is a locative phrase 'in the world' (اندر = in, جهان = world/life).
segments.s444-g20: بدو تازه بد روزگار مهان — 'بدو' is the dative clitic/phrase 'to him' (indirect object). 'تازه بد' is a compressed poetic adjective phrase meaning 'newly bad' or 'a fresh misfortune'; 'روزگار' here means 'fate/time', and 'مهان' (literally 'guests' in the lexicon given) functions as a genitival/attributive plural meaning 'of the great/guests' — together conveying 'a fresh, ill fate befell (him) concerning the great/guests.' Word order is inverted (adjectives and modifiers appear before the head in poetry).
segments.s444-g21: به زابلستان زان نشان کشته شد — 'به زابلستان' gives the location 'in/to Zabulistan'. 'زان' = 'from that/for that reason' (referring to the omen/mark just mentioned). 'نشان' = 'sign/omen' (the cause), and 'کشته شد' is the passive 'was killed' (شد as auxiliary marking the passive/result). The clause means 'because of that sign he was killed in Zabulistan.'
segments.s444-g22: ز دردش دد و دام سرگشته شد — 'ز' (poetic form of از) = 'from/because of', 'دردش' = 'his pain' (درد + enclitic ش = his). 'دد' here is an agentive plural 'enemies/hostile ones' (or wild beasts in some contexts), 'و دام' = 'and snares/traps', 'سرگشته شد' = 'became bewildered/astounded' (شد as 'became'). The clause says that from his suffering enemies and traps were left bewildered/confused.
segments.s444-g23: همانا که بر خون اسفندیار — 'همانا' is an emphatic 'verily/behold'. 'که' introduces the clause. 'بر' = 'over/on' indicating the object of mourning, 'خون' = 'blood' (here 'the bloodshed'), and 'اسفندیار' is the proper name; together: 'verily, over Esfandiyār's blood...' (setting up the scene of mourning).
segments.s444-g24: به زاری بگرید به ایوان نگار — 'به زاری' = 'with lamentation/in mourning' (به + noun); 'بگرید' is a third-person narrative-form of 'to weep/cry' (here rendered as 'wailed/weeped' — a vivid narrative present/past reading is possible in classical Persian). 'به ایوان نگار' gives the locus 'at/to the painted/ornamented iwan' (ایوان = hall/porch, نگار here = 'painted/ornamented' or 'picture/decoration'). So: 'the painted hall wailed in lamentation.'
segments.s444-g1: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. The phrase follows a classical Persian order: preposition به + object تخت نیا (’the ancestral throne’) plus the verb برنشست (بر + نشست) where برنشست is a compound indicating 'sat upon'. نیا is genitively linked to تخت ('throne of the ancestor').
segments.s444-g2: This is a coordinated pair of past actions: 'کمر ... بست' (he bound/girded his waist) uses کمر as the body-part object, with the instrument expressed by با میان ('with a belt'). The conjunction و links the next verb phrase; بگشاد is an archaic/perfective verbal form (ب- + گشاد) meaning 'opened' and takes دست as its object ('opened/extended his hand').
segments.s444-g3: سپه را uses the object marker را to mark سپه ('the army') as the definite recipient. درم داد and دینار داد are two coordinated past actions (داد repeated) listing what was given; و simply connects the two types of coin.
segments.s444-g4: همان is a demonstrative ('that' or 'the same') modifying کشور. The noun مرز follows with بسیار functioning postpositively as a quantifier ('many' / 'much'). The single verb داد governs both nouns, meaning 'granted/gave'.
segments.s444-g5: یکی here has the sense 'one' / 'a certain' and modifies انجمن ('an assembly' or 'council'). ساخت is the past verb 'made/constructed', and از بخردان is a prepositional phrase 'from the wise', indicating the composition of that assembly.
segments.s444-g6: This phrase lists the members of the assembly: بزرگان ('great/elder men') and a second coordinated group. کار آزموده is an adjectival compound ('experienced, tried in work') modifying the following noun ردان. The exact nuance of ردان is specialized/archaic here (see senses).
segments.s444-g7: چنین گفت is a conventional formula meaning 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus'; گفت = past 'said'). کز is the fused/poetic form of 'که از' meaning 'that from' or 'that concerning', and کار اسفندیار is a genitive phrase 'the deeds of Esfandiar' serving as the topic of the following clause.
segments.s444-g8: ز is the poetic/prepositional form 'from'; نیک و بد is a standard binary 'good and bad'; گردش روزگار is a noun phrase 'the turning/cycle of days/fate' where گردش ('turn, course') is head and روزگار ('time, fate') qualifies it — together meaning 'turns of fate' or 'the course of events'.
segments.s444-g9: A direct address: 'همه یاد دارید' literally 'all (of you) have memory' = 'do you all remember'. Persian places the possessor/subject before the verbal phrase; 'یاد داشتن' (to have memory) functions like 'to remember' and here is in the present (دارید) addressing the audience. 'پیر و جوان' are nouns used as categories ('the old and the young') joined by و.
segments.s444-g10: 'هرانکس که هستید' is a free relative: 'whoever you are' (هرانکس = whoever; که introduces the clause; هستید is 2nd‑person plural present 'you are'). 'روشن‌روان' is an epithet placed after the clause and addresses the audience ('bright‑soul' / 'clear‑souled'), functioning as a vocative/description linked to 'هرانکس که هستید'.
segments.s444-g11: An embedded relative clause introduced by که linking back to 'remember': 'که رستم گه زندگانی چه کرد' = 'what Rostam at one time did in his life'. Word order is S O V (رستم … چه کرد), with گه as an adverb 'once/at times' modifying the action, چه functioning as the interrogative 'what' inside the reported clause, and کرد in the past tense 'did'.
segments.s444-g12: A nominal apposition/resumption naming the agent of the remembered deeds: 'همان زال افسونگر آن پیرمرد' = 'that same Zal, the enchanter, that old man.' همان ('that same') points back to the preceding clause; names and epithets are stacked in apposition for emphasis.
segments.s444-g13: The clause sets up the exception object: 'فرامرز جز کین ما در جهان' = 'Faramarz, except our vengeance in the world…'. جز + noun expresses 'except' — here 'جز کین ما' = 'except our vengeance' and در جهان gives the spatial/domain frame 'in the world'.
segments.s444-g14: نجوید here is the present‑tense form of جستن with the negative prefix ن‑; in this classical construction 'X جز Y نجوید' means 'X seeks nothing except Y' (so the negative is combined with the exception). همی is a continuous/adverbial particle 'continually/always'. آشکار و نهان are adverbial/qualifying opposites 'openly and secretly' modifying how he seeks vengeance.
segments.s444-g15: Two parallel nominative phrases: 'سرم پر ز دردست' and 'دل پر ز خون' = 'my head full of pain and my heart full of blood.' پر ز + noun is the poetic way to say 'full of (X)'. 'سرم' is the possessive form 'my head' and 'دل' 'heart'; the middle item 'دردست' is an archaic/poetic form here functioning to express 'pain' (the line uses compressed/older morphology), so the whole phrase reads 'my head is full of pain.'
segments.s444-g16: 'جز از کین ندارم به مغز اندرون' = 'I have nothing in the inner brain/mind except (my) vengeance.' جز از + noun marks the sole exception; ندارم is 1st‑person present negative 'I do not have' (here 'I have nothing but…'). به مغز اندرون is a locative/figurative phrase 'in the inner brain/within my mind', with اندرون an archaic Persian word for 'inside/within.'
segments.s444-g57: This is a verbless predicative clause: ز + NP + pronoun + comparative adjective. ز (poetic for از) marks the basis or source ('by/from your past deeds'), تو is the pronoun possessor, and داناتری is the comparative adjective 'wiser' predicated of 'you'. Word order (source before pronoun then adjective) is typical in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s444-g58: Another comparative clause parallel to the first. ز introduces the standard comparative phrase ('than/from the warlike men'), مردان جنگی = 'warlike men, warriors', and تواناتری is the comparative 'more able/stronger' predicated of 'you'. The sentence omits an explicit than-clause after the adjective because the preceding prepositional phrase supplies the comparison.
segments.s444-g59: به گیتی sets the locus ‘in/for the world’. همان کن is an imperative: همان ('that very/exactly so') + کن (do!), so 'do exactly that'. که introduces a relative/subordinate clause ('which/that'), and کام آیدت literally 'the desire comes to you' = 'which satisfies you' or 'which your desire attains'. آیدت is a present/factual form 'comes to you'.
segments.s444-g60: وگر = 'and/but if' introduces a conditional. زان ('from that') refers back to the preceding act/word, سخن here means 'word/act/saying', فر و نام are two connected nouns ('royal glory' and 'name' = renown), and آیدت 'come to you'. The whole clause means 'and if from that word glory and fame befall you'.
segments.s444-g61: نپیچد is a negative optative/prohibitive form ('may ... not turn' / 'let ... not turn'); Persian commonly places the verb before the subject (نپیچد کسی). سر ز فرمان تو is an idiom 'turn one's head from your command' = 'disobey/turn away from your order', where ز marks separation/from.
segments.s444-g62: که introduces a reason or consequence ('for/that/so that'); یارد here is 'companion/ally', گذشتن ز پیمان تو uses گذشتن in the sense 'to go past/abandon' (i.e., break or depart from a pact), with ز marking 'from' and پیمان تو 'your covenant'. The clause explains the danger being guarded against: a companion abandoning the oath.
segments.s444-g63: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. پاسخ چنین = 'such an answer' (پاسخ 'reply/answer', چنین 'such/so'), یافت is the past tense 'he found/received', and از لشکرش indicates the source 'from his army' (لشکر with the possessive suffix ش 'his').
segments.s444-g64: This clause states the result: به کین اندرون 'to/into hatred within' (i.e., 'inner hatred/anger'), تیزتر شد is comparative past 'became sharper/keener', and سرش ('his head') is used idiomatically for his inner feeling or mind; ش is the possessive suffix 'his'. The verb شد is the copula 'became'.
segments.s444-g25: هم is an emphatic adverb meaning “also/too” placed at the start to link this line to the previous thought. از + noun (از خون) is a prepositional phrase indicating origin or source (‘from the blood’); آن نامداران is a definite NP ‘those famed ones,’ and the independent pronoun ما following the noun marks possession/classical ‘our’ (a postnominal possessive common in epic Persian). The whole phrase functions as an origin/agent phrase that will be predicated of the following noun phrase.
segments.s444-g26: This is a coordinated noun phrase: جوانان “young men/ youths” + و “and” + جنگی سواران “warlike horsemen/riders.” The final ما is again the postnominal possessive ‘our,’ echoed for emphasis and rhythm; in context it attaches to the coordinated NP (the repetition is rhetorical). Word order (noun + ما) is typical in older poetic Persian.
segments.s444-g27: هر آنکس که ... is a relative clause ‘whoever/anyone who…’. او باشد is the third‑person form of بودن here meaning ‘he is/is made’ (classical present/subjunctive form used in relative clauses). از آب پاک is literal ‘from pure water’ used idiomatically for ‘of pure birth/lineage’ or moral purity; the preposition phrase modifies the preceding predicate.
segments.s444-g28: نیارد is an archaic/poetic form corresponding to نیاورد (from آوردن) meaning ‘(he) will not bring / does not bring’; here it functions as the main verb of the clause (third‑person). سر گوهر اندر مغاک is a prepositional/direct object phrase ‘the jewel‑head into the abyss’ (سر گوهر = ‘the jewel’s/headlike gem’), with اندر = ‘into/in’ and مغاک = ‘abyss/pit’. Together the phrase says that such a person will not cast/place the precious head into the abyss.
segments.s444-g29: به کردار is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘in manner/behavior’ (به used to form a manner phrase). شاه آفریدون is the reference NP ‘King Afrīdūn’ in apposition/standard comparison. بود is the past copula ‘was’—the clause states a past characteristic: ‘he was in the conduct of King Afrīdūn’ (i.e. he behaved like him).
segments.s444-g30: چو introduces a temporal/comparative clause ‘when/as’: چو خونین بباشد = ‘when/if he is blood‑stained.’ باشد here is the present/subjunctive form of ‘to be’ used in the subordinate clause, and the main clause then uses بود as the assertive past copula: همایون بود = ‘he was auspicious/august.’ The two verbs reflect classical poetic layering of mood (subordinate present form + main past assertion).
segments.s444-g31: که introduces an explanatory/causal clause ‘for/that’. ضحاک را places Zahhāk as the topicalized agent (را here marks a definite/topic object or emphasis in classical style). از پی خون جم is a prepositional phrase ‘in pursuit of (after) Jam’s blood’—از پی = ‘after/in pursuit of’, خون = ‘blood’, جم = the proper name ‘Jam’. Together this sets Zahhāk as acting because of Jam’s blood.
segments.s444-g32: ز (poetic form of از) + نام‌آوران جهان = ‘of the world’s renowned ones’ (object/affected group). کرد کم is a compound verbal idea: کرد (do/make) + کم (few) = ‘made few’ or ‘diminished’—i.e. ‘he reduced the number of the world’s famed heroes.’ Word order places the affected NP before the verbal phrase for emphasis, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s444-g41: فرامرز is the proper name (subject). کز is the archaic/poetic relative 'که + ز' meaning 'who/that' and introduces a relative clause. بهر here means 'for' or 'for the sake of' and takes the following noun خون پدر ('the blood of [his] father') as its object; note Persian omits the possessive suffix here, so پدر is read as 'his father.'
segments.s444-g42: به + noun phrase (به خورشید تابان) indicates direction/aim ('toward the shining sun'). تابان is an adjectival/postpositive modifier 'shining' following خورشید. برآورد is a past-tense verb (3rd singular) formed from برآوردن meaning 'to raise/bring up' (poetic present here), and سر ('head') is its object; poetic inversion places the object after the verb.
segments.s444-g43: به کابل شد literally 'became to Kabul' and here functions as 'he arrived/went to Kabul' (شد as the past-tense auxiliary/motion verb). و is the copulative 'and'. کین رستم بخواست is an inverted order where رستم is the subject and کین the object ('Rostam sought vengeance'); بخواست is past tense of خواستن 'to desire/seek.'
segments.s444-g44: همه بوم و بر is a binomial meaning 'all towns and lands / everywhere' (همه 'entire/whole' modifies the paired nouns). کرد is the past tense 'did/made' and here functions like 'laid low / devastated.' با is the instrumental 'with' and خاک راست ('earth' + راست 'straight/level') expresses that he leveled things flat with the ground; راست modifies خاک ('straight/level to the earth'). Note بر in the fixed phrase بوم و بر is a lexical partner of بوم rather than the prepositional sense.
segments.s444-g45: زمین را is the definite object ('the land'). ز here means 'because of / from' and links to خون ('blood') as the cause. بازنشناختند is a poetic past-form meaning 'they recognized (again)' but in context conveys 'they could no longer recognize' or 'they did not recognize [it] any more' because the ground was soaked with blood; grammatically it is 3rd-person plural past.
segments.s444-g46: همی is an adverb of continuity/emphasis ('continually/again'). باره here conveys an instance/assault sense (a burst/raid). بر کشتگان is a prepositional phrase 'upon the slain' (کشتگان = 'the slain'), and تاختند is the 3rd-person plural past of تاختن, here meaning 'they attacked/charged.'
segments.s444-g47: به کینه is a prepositional phrase 'deserving of hatred' (به + noun). سزاوارتر is the comparative form of سزاوار ('deserving'), so سزاوارتر کس منم literally 'I am the person more deserving' — word order is inverted for emphasis (کس 'person' followed by منم 'I am').
segments.s444-g48: که introduces a purpose or result clause here ('that / so that'). بر شیر درنده is a prepositional phrase 'against/upon the ferocious lion' (شیر = 'lion', درنده = 'ferocious'). اسپ is 'steed/horse' and افگنم is a poetic verb form expressing the speaker's intended action ('that I may throw/bring down / lay low'); the exact lexical sense of افگنم is archaic/poetic and not provided in the listed senses, so it is left as an open/irregular verbal form here.

sentences.s444-s33: They arrayed the whole of Sistan; they set themselves upon these and rose up.
sentences.s444-s34: At dusk the sound of the war-drum rose; around the host the heavens turned ebony.
sentences.s444-s35: From that renowned host there rode forth a hundred thousand sword-wielding horsemen.
sentences.s444-s25: If you reckon among the world's famed horsemen, you will not see a rider like Esfandiyār.
sentences.s444-s26: See what he sees, and what answer you will give to this; strive so that you may put Farrokh's counsel into place.
sentences.s444-s27: When the host heard Bahman's speech, whoever is a well-wisher of the king ...
sentences.s444-s28: They said aloud, 'We are your servants' — our hearts are all filled with love for you.
sentences.s444-s17: Manuchehr, with Salm and the mighty Tur, brought a great army from Amol.
sentences.s444-s18: He went to Chin (China) and the ancestral vengeance demanded retribution; to me, that is indeed a true tale.
sentences.s444-s19: When Keykhosrow came from Afrasiab, he made the world, by bloodshed, like a sea of water.
sentences.s444-s20: My father came and demanded vengeance on Lohrasp; he piled the slain upon the earth like a straight/upright mountain.
sentences.s444-s9: Two warriors, like Nush-āzar and Mehrnush, from whose pain a cry arose.
sentences.s444-s10: Like Esfandiyār — in the world to him a fresh, evil fate for the great befell.
sentences.s444-s11: In Zabulistan, because of that sign he was slain; from his pain enemies and snares were left bewildered.
sentences.s444-s12: Truly — that, for Esfandiyār's blood, the painted iwan wailed in lamentation.
sentences.s444-s1: When Bahman sat upon the ancestral throne, he girded his waist with a belt and opened his hand.
sentences.s444-s2: He gave the army dirhams and dinars, and granted that country and many frontiers.
sentences.s444-s3: He formed one assembly from the wise: great men and the experienced ردان.
sentences.s444-s4: Thus he said that from Esfandiar's deeds would come both good and bad turns of fate.
sentences.s444-s5: Remember this, all of you — old and young; whoever you are, bright‑souled.
sentences.s444-s6: — what Rostam at one time did in life — that same Zal, the enchanter, that old man.
sentences.s444-s7: Faramarz seeks nothing in the world except our vengeance, openly and covertly.
sentences.s444-s8: My head is full of pain and my heart full of blood; I have nothing in my inner mind except vengeance.
sentences.s444-s29: You are wiser by your past deeds, and more able than fighting men.
sentences.s444-s30: Do in the world exactly what satisfies you; and if from that word glory and renown come to you.
sentences.s444-s31: Let no one turn his head away from your command, lest a comrade abandon your covenant.
sentences.s444-s32: When he received such an answer from his army, inward hatred (or anger) grew keener in him.
sentences.s444-s13: Also from the blood of those renowned ones of ours are our youths and our warlike horsemen.
sentences.s444-s14: Whoever is of pure water (i.e. of pure lineage), will not cast the jewel‑head into the abyss.
sentences.s444-s15: He acted like King Afrīdūn; when blood‑stained he was august (auspicious).
sentences.s444-s16: For Zahhāk, in pursuit of Jam’s blood, thinned out the world’s renowned men.
sentences.s444-s21: Faramarz, who, for his father's blood, raised his head toward the shining sun.
sentences.s444-s22: He went to Kabul, and Rostam sought vengeance; he laid all towns and lands level with the earth.
sentences.s444-s23: They could no longer recognize the land because of the blood; again and again they fell upon the slain.
sentences.s444-s24: I am the man most deserving of hatred, that I may fling my steed upon the ferocious lion.
